refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety. Refining the FR to explicitly reference the acceptance criterion (≤1%) and network conditions scope clarifies the measurable target and boundary conditions. High safety criticality demands precise definition.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under agreed network conditions (latency profile, bandwidth, interference scenarios) using telemetry capture and statistical analysis. Acceptance: measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined limit across representative operational scenarios.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall detect and report packet loss events in real-time.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall implement error recovery and retransmission mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall adapt communication parameters (e.g., compression, frequency) based on network conditions to maintain packet loss within limits.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The teleoperation system shall provide operator feedback when packet loss exceeds safe thresholds.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant communication channels (dual network paths) with automatic failover to reduce single-point-of-failure packet loss.
DP-B: Deploy adaptive quality-of-service (QoS) management with dynamic codec selection, frame rate adjustment, and priority queuing to maintain packet loss within limits.
DP-C: Integrate forward error correction (FEC) and selective retransmission protocols at the transport layer to recover lost packets without excessive latency.
DP-D: Combine DP-B and DP-C: adaptive QoS management coupled with FEC/retransmission to provide layered resilience.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a multi-layer communication resilience strategy combining adaptive QoS management (dynamic codec selection, frame rate adjustment, priority queuing) with forward error correction and selective retransmission protocols. This approach addresses packet loss at both the application layer (QoS adaptation) and transport layer (FEC/retransmission), providing defense-in-depth suitable for high-safety-criticality teleoperation.

potential_sources: QoS adaptation logic must couple with telemetry data collection (network condition monitoring) to inform codec/frame-rate decisions.
FEC/retransmission mechanisms couple with the underlying Network Link interface to ensure compatibility with customer network infrastructure.
Operator feedback (SafetyAlert interface) must couple with packet loss detection to alert operators when thresholds are exceeded.
coupling_check.notes: Couplings are manageable through well-defined interfaces (Telemetry for network metrics, SafetyAlert for operator notification, Network Link for transport-layer protocol negotiation). No circular dependencies detected. DP-D is decoupled from specific hardware platforms, supporting both wired and wireless network conditions.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer network infrastructure supports standard transport protocols (TCP/UDP) and allows QoS tagging.
ASSUMPTION: Operator latency tolerance is compatible with FEC/retransmission overhead (typically 50–200 ms additional delay).
RISK: Aggressive FEC overhead may reduce effective bandwidth; requires tuning per network profile.
RISK: Adaptive QoS may degrade video/sensor quality below operator situational awareness threshold if network severely degrades; mitigation via SafetyAlert feedback.
RISK: Implementation complexity increases testing and validation burden; high-safety-criticality context demands rigorous protocol verification.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-D is sound but should be refined to explicitly define: (1) QoS adaptation thresholds and decision logic (e.g., trigger codec downgrade if packet loss > 0.5%), (2) FEC redundancy ratio (e.g., 10–20% overhead), (3) retransmission timeout and retry limits, and (4) operator alert criteria. These parameters must be customer-configurable and validated against agreed network conditions.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module encapsulates all communication resilience logic, including adaptive QoS management, FEC encoding/decoding, selective retransmission, and packet loss monitoring. It interfaces with the Vehicle module (via Tasking for control commands and Telemetry for sensor data) and Safety Services (via SafetyAlert for operator notifications). The module owns the transport-layer protocol stack and QoS decision engine.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional flow of sensor data (vehicle state, network metrics) from Vehicle to Teleoperation, and network condition feedback from Teleoperation to Vehicle for adaptive behavior.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operator commands (control inputs) received by Teleoperation from remote operator console; subject to QoS adaptation and retransmission logic.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Packet loss threshold breach notifications sent from Teleoperation to Safety Services and operator console to trigger safety interventions (e.g., autonomous halt, operator warning).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Physical/logical network connection managed by Teleoperation; includes protocol negotiation, QoS tagging, and FEC/retransmission at transport layer.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level container but does not own the detailed communication resilience logic. Packet loss management is a specialized concern best owned by Teleoperation. Assigning to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility and obscure ownership of QoS/FEC implementation.

Vehicle module focuses on physical equipment control and sensor acquisition. Communication resilience is orthogonal to vehicle mechanics and should not be embedded in Vehicle to avoid coupling vehicle firmware with network protocol logic. High diffusion risk if packet loss handling is scattered across vehicle subsystems.

Teleoperation is the natural owner of communication quality management. It already owns the operator-to-vehicle command/feedback loop and is responsible for ensuring communication fidelity. QoS adaptation, FEC, and retransmission are core teleoperation concerns. Minimal diffusion risk; clear ownership boundary.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alerts but should not own the communication resilience mechanisms themselves. Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert notifications from Teleoperation when packet loss thresholds are breached, triggering safety responses. Assigning packet loss management to Safety Services would blur the distinction between communication quality and safety policy.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-D (adaptive QoS + FEC/retransmission). It has clear functional scope (remote operation), owns the communication pathway, and can implement QoS logic without diffusing responsibility across vehicle or safety subsystems. Interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and align with Teleoperation's existing boundaries.
containment_risks: RISK: Teleoperation module may become complex if QoS adaptation logic is not cleanly separated from command/feedback processing. Mitigation: decompose into sub-modules (QoS Manager, FEC Codec, Retransmission Handler) at the next design level.

RISK: Network Link interface abstraction must hide platform-specific details (e.g., WiFi vs. cellular vs. wired); if not properly abstracted, Teleoperation becomes tightly coupled to network hardware. Mitigation: define Network Link as a standardized interface contract.

RISK: SafetyAlert interface must be non-blocking and real-time; if Teleoperation's packet loss detection is slow, operator alerts may be delayed. Mitigation: implement packet loss monitoring as a high-priority, low-latency task within Teleoperation.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Teleoperation module has sufficient autonomy and resources (CPU, memory, network bandwidth) to implement DP-D without impacting other teleoperation functions (e.g., video streaming, command latency). Define sub-module boundaries (QoS Manager, FEC Codec, Retransmission Handler) to manage complexity. Establish clear interface contracts for Telemetry (network metrics schema), Tasking (command priority levels), SafetyAlert (alert latency SLA), and Network Link (protocol version negotiation).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (adaptive QoS + FEC/retransmission) directly addresses the refined FR requirement to maintain packet loss ≤ customer-defined threshold. The multi-layer approach (application-level QoS adaptation + transport-level FEC/retransmission) provides the necessary resilience mechanisms. Assumptions (standard transport protocols, operator latency tolerance) are reasonable and documented. Risks (FEC overhead, quality degradation, implementation complexity) are identified and mitigable.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module ownership is clear and non-diffuse. QoS/FEC logic is naturally contained within the teleoperation communication pathway.
Sub-module decomposition (QoS Manager, FEC Codec, Retransmission Handler) must be defined at the next level to prevent internal diffusion within Teleoperation.

Network Link interface abstraction is critical; if not properly defined, Teleoperation may become tightly coupled to specific network hardware, increasing diffusion risk across future platform variants.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module has a containment score of 0.95 for DP-D. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are all known and well-defined. No new interfaces required. Ownership boundary is clear: Teleoperation owns communication resilience; Vehicle owns sensor/control hardware; Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-009 is feasible via packet loss measurement under agreed network conditions. Test scenarios should include nominal conditions, degraded network (high latency, jitter, packet loss), and network recovery. Acceptance criteria (≤1% packet loss) are measurable and objective. Teleoperation module must expose packet loss metrics via Telemetry interface for verification.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module is responsible for implementing and maintaining packet loss ≤ customer-defined threshold. Vehicle module provides network condition telemetry. Safety Services responds to SafetyAlert notifications when thresholds are breached. Operator is responsible for safe operation within communication quality constraints.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to specify QoS adaptation thresholds, FEC redundancy ratios, retransmission timeout/retry limits, and operator alert criteria. These parameters must be customer-configurable.
Define sub-module boundaries within Teleoperation (QoS Manager, FEC Codec, Retransmission Handler) to manage implementation complexity and enable independent testing.

Establish formal interface contracts for Telemetry (network metrics schema), Tasking (command priority levels), SafetyAlert (alert latency SLA ≤ 100 ms), and Network Link (protocol version negotiation, QoS tagging support).
Conduct risk assessment on FEC overhead impact on effective bandwidth and operator situational awareness. Define fallback strategies if network severely degrades.
Plan for high-safety-criticality validation: protocol verification, fault injection testing, and operator acceptance testing under representative network conditions.

decision.justification: The refined FR-NGHE-009 is well-defined and measurable. DP-D (adaptive QoS + FEC/retransmission) is a sound, multi-layer solution with manageable coupling and documented assumptions/risks. Teleoperation module is the clear, non-diffuse owner with a containment score of 0.95. All gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass. The next step is to decompose DP-D into sub-FRs and sub-DPs at the next level to address implementation complexity and define detailed QoS adaptation logic, FEC codec selection, and retransmission protocols. High-safety-criticality context demands rigorous decomposition and validation planning.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The Teleoperation module shall detect packet loss in real-time and report metrics (loss rate, burst patterns) via the Telemetry interface.

FR-NGHE-009.2: The Teleoperation module shall implement adaptive QoS management that adjusts codec, frame rate, and priority queuing based on detected network conditions to maintain packet loss within limits.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The Teleoperation module shall implement forward error correction (FEC) encoding/decoding with configurable redundancy to recover lost packets without excessive latency overhead.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The Teleoperation module shall implement selective retransmission with timeout and retry limits to recover critical lost packets (e.g., safety-critical commands).
FR-NGHE-009.5: The Teleoperation module shall alert the operator and Safety Services via SafetyAlert when packet loss exceeds safe thresholds, with latency ≤ 100 ms.

assumptions: Customer network infrastructure supports standard transport protocols (TCP/UDP) and QoS tagging.
Operator latency tolerance is compatible with FEC/retransmission overhead (50–200 ms additional delay).
Agreed network conditions are defined and documented by customer (e.g., latency profile, bandwidth, interference scenarios).
Teleoperation module has sufficient computational resources (CPU, memory) to implement adaptive QoS and FEC without impacting other teleoperation functions.
SafetyAlert interface supports non-blocking, real-time notifications with latency ≤ 100 ms.
Vehicle module provides network condition telemetry (latency, jitter, packet loss) to Teleoperation for QoS adaptation decisions.
open_risks: FEC overhead may reduce effective bandwidth; requires tuning per network profile and validation against customer network conditions.
Adaptive QoS may degrade video/sensor quality below operator situational awareness threshold if network severely degrades; mitigation via SafetyAlert feedback and fallback strategies.
Implementation complexity increases testing and validation burden; high-safety-criticality context demands rigorous protocol verification, fault injection testing, and operator acceptance testing.
Network Link interface abstraction must hide platform-specific details; if not properly abstracted, Teleoperation becomes tightly coupled to network hardware.
Sub-module decomposition within Teleoperation must prevent internal diffusion; clear ownership of QoS Manager, FEC Codec, and Retransmission Handler is required.
evidence_needed: Customer-defined packet loss acceptance criterion and agreed network conditions (latency profile, bandwidth, interference scenarios).
Operator latency tolerance specification and situational awareness requirements (e.g., video frame rate, control responsiveness).
Network Link interface specification (protocol version, QoS tagging support, failover mechanisms).
Telemetry interface schema for network metrics (latency, jitter, packet loss rate, burst patterns).
SafetyAlert interface specification (alert types, latency SLA, operator feedback mechanisms).
FEC codec selection criteria and redundancy ratio tuning guidelines.
Retransmission timeout and retry limit specifications for different packet types (critical vs. non-critical).
High-safety-criticality validation plan (protocol verification, fault injection testing, operator acceptance testing).
